s: A river captain's guilt trip became country music's holiest hall
w: Thomas Ryman owned saloons and a riverboat fleet; he walked into a revival tent in 1885 to heckle the preacher and left a convert, pledging to build him an indoor tabernacle. The Union Gospel Tabernacle opened in 1892, renamed after Ryman's death. The Grand Ole Opry broadcast from this room 1943-1974 — the pews are still the seats.
_facts: Opened 1892 as Union Gospel Tabernacle, built by riverboat entrepreneur Thomas Ryman after religious conversion.
Home of the Grand Ole Opry from 1943 to 1974.
Named National Historic Landmark 2001.
Designated a Rock & Roll Hall of Fame Landmark in 2022.

s: The little room on Music Row where Elvis, Dolly and Chet invented a sound
w: Chet Atkins and Steve Sholes opened this 30-by-50-foot room in 1957 and it became the engine of the Nashville Sound — smooth strings replacing raw twang, saving country radio from rock and roll. RCA Studio B produced 60 percent of all Billboard country hits in its two decades of operation. Elvis cut around 200 tracks here. Closed 1977; tours run through the Country Music Hall of Fame.
_facts: Established 1957 by Steve Sholes and Chet Atkins for RCA Victor.
Produced 60% of Billboard country chart hits during its operation.
Elvis Presley recorded approximately 200 songs here.
Studio closed 1977; now operated as a museum by the Country Music Hall of Fame since 1992.

s: Nashville's oldest boneyard, where Old Glory got her name
w: Opened 1822 and packed with the city's founders, four Confederate generals, and the man who put a name to the American flag. Sea captain William Driver hoisted a large stars-and-stripes in Salem, Massachusetts in 1831 and dubbed it 'Old Glory' — he's buried here, as are two of the original Fisk Jubilee Singers. 22,000 souls on 5 acres.
_facts: Nashville's oldest public cemetery, opened January 1, 1822.
Sea captain William Driver, who coined the name 'Old Glory' for the US flag in 1831, is buried here.
Two original Fisk Jubilee Singers (Mabel Imes and Ella Sheppard) are interred here.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places 1972.

s: A full-scale concrete replica of the Athens Parthenon, in a city park
w: Nashville called itself 'Athens of the South' and for the 1897 Tennessee Centennial Exposition actually built the Parthenon — exact dimensions, plaster then concrete. The only building from that fair they couldn't bear to tear down. Inside stands a 41-foot gilded Athena, re-created by sculptor Alan LeQuire in 1990 from archaeological records. The British Museum refused to loan the Elgin Marbles; plaster casts fill the west room instead.
_facts: Full-scale replica of the Parthenon in Athens, built 1897 for the Tennessee Centennial Exposition.
Original structure was plaster and wood; rebuilt in concrete 1920-1931.
Houses a 41-foot gilded Athena Parthenos statue sculpted by Alan LeQuire, completed 1990.
Centennial Park is the site of what was originally the 1897 world's fair.

s: Maxwell House coffee built this Georgian mansion; the family sold up and gave it away
w: Joel Cheek blended a coffee for Nashville's Maxwell House Hotel, sold the brand to Postum Cereals in 1928 for $40 million, and his cousin Leslie used the windfall to build this 55-acre Georgian limestone estate. Leslie died two years after moving in. His daughter eventually gave it to the city as a botanical garden and art museum, which opened 1960. The grounds are now the most visited gardens in Tennessee.
_facts: Estate built 1932 with proceeds from the $40 million sale of Maxwell House Coffee to Postum Cereals.
Designed by New York architect Bryant Fleming; 30,000 sq ft Georgian mansion.
Opened as a botanical garden and art museum in 1960.
55-acre estate on the western edge of Nashville.

s: Nashville's dive-bar cathedral of bluegrass — cash-only, no reserved seats
w: A cinder-block box near Music Row with no stage lighting to speak of, and widely considered one of the finest bluegrass venues on earth. Bill Monroe, Ricky Skaggs, Alison Krauss, Dolly Parton, and Ralph Stanley have all played here. Mel Gibson and U2 have sat in the crowd. Owner J.T. Gray was inducted into the Bluegrass Music Hall of Fame in 2020. Show up early.
_facts: Small concert venue near Music Row dedicated to bluegrass music.
Notable performers include Bill Monroe, Alison Krauss, Dolly Parton, and Ralph Stanley.
Owner J.T. Gray inducted into the Bluegrass Music Hall of Fame in 2020.
Known for its stripped-down, no-frills atmosphere.

s: The Man in Black's entire life in one downtown building — world's largest Cash collection
w: Opened 2013 and officially authorised by the Cash estate, this is the most comprehensive single-artist collection in country music — stage clothes, handwritten lyrics, the original June Carter letters, and a stone wall transplanted from Cash's lake house in Hendersonville. The museum holds more than 800 artefacts and sells out most days. Walk through the door in black.
_facts: Opened May 2013 in downtown Nashville; officially authorised by Johnny Cash's estate.
Houses the world's largest collection of Johnny Cash memorabilia.
Includes a stone wall removed from Cash's lake house in Hendersonville, Tennessee.
More than 800 artefacts on display.

s: Tennessee's tallest skyscraper has two antennae that look exactly like Batman's ears
w: AT&T's 617-foot glass tower completed in 1994 is officially called 333 Commerce Street, but no Nashville resident calls it that. The twin rooftop antennae mimic Batman's cowl so precisely that the nickname was inevitable — and it stuck. Tallest building in Tennessee, yet its most famous quality is looking like a comic-book villain at dusk. The city officially uses 'Batman Building' in tourism materials.
_facts: 617-foot, 33-story AT&T skyscraper at 333 Commerce Street, completed 1994.
Currently the tallest building in Tennessee.
Nicknamed 'Batman Building' by locals for its twin rooftop antennae resembling Batman's cowl.
Designed by Earl Swensson & Associates.

s: The architect who designed it died before it finished — and is entombed inside its walls
w: William Strickland of Philadelphia, who called the Greek Revival statehouse his greatest achievement, died in 1854 with the building still unfinished. The commission entombed him in the northeast wall. The Capitol was completed in 1859 by convict and enslaved labour. James K. Polk and his wife Sarah are buried on the grounds. One of 11 state capitols without a dome; one of the oldest working statehouses in the country.
_facts: Designed by architect William Strickland; built 1845-1859 using convict and enslaved labour.
Strickland died before completion and was entombed within the building's northeast wall.
James K. Polk (11th US President) and wife Sarah Childress Polk are buried on the grounds.
National Historic Landmark since 1971; one of 11 US state capitols without a dome.

s: Fisk students sang their way across Europe to fund this building — and it shows
w: In 1871 Fisk University was broke. Its nine-student choir set out to raise money performing spirituals for white Northern audiences, eventually crossing the Atlantic and singing before Queen Victoria, who wept and commissioned a portrait. The tour raised enough to build Jubilee Hall, completed 1876 — Fisk's first permanent building, a six-storey Gothic Revival landmark. Victoria's floor-to-ceiling portrait of the singers still hangs inside.
_facts: Completed 1876 as Fisk University's first permanent building; Gothic Revival architecture.
Built with proceeds from the Fisk Jubilee Singers' 1871-1873 European fundraising tour.
Queen Victoria commissioned a portrait of the original Jubilee Singers, still displayed inside.
Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1974.

s: A 90-seat strip-mall restaurant where Garth Brooks was discovered in 1987
w: Amy Kurland opened this in a nondescript Green Hills strip mall in 1982; within years it had become the songwriter's room — performers play 'in the round,' facing each other rather than the audience, so every word lands. Garth Brooks was spotted here in 1987 by Capitol Records before he had a deal. Taylor Swift played it as a teenager. The TV show 'Nashville' set its signature scene here. Ninety seats. Book early.
_facts: Founded by Amy Kurland in 1982 as a 90-seat cafe and music venue in Green Hills.
Known for its 'in the round' performance format where songwriters face each other.
Garth Brooks was discovered here in 1987 by Capitol Records.
Featured prominently in the television drama 'Nashville'.

s: A celebrated thoroughbred stud farm built on enslaved labour — the full story is now told
w: At its peak this Greek Revival estate covered 5,400 acres worked by 136 enslaved men and women, and its stables produced Iroquois, the first American-bred horse to win the Epsom Derby (1881). Union soldiers looted the mansion during the Civil War while the owner sat in prison. Roughly half the formerly enslaved workforce returned as paid employees after 1865. Since the 1990s the site has made their stories central.
_facts: Historic farm established 1807; Greek Revival mansion built 1853.
Used 136 enslaved workers at its peak; grew to 5,400 acres.
Bred Iroquois, the first American-bred horse to win the Epsom Derby in 1881.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places; now a museum and winery on 30 remaining acres.

s: Grand Ole Opry played here in the 1930s before the Ryman — the format it uses today was born in this room
w: The Belcourt opened in 1925 as a silent-movie house and spent the 1930s doubling as an Opry stage — from 1934 to 1936 the show had to split each broadcast into two performances because the room was too small for one crowd. That two-set format never left. The oldest operating cinema in Tennessee is now a beloved nonprofit art-house, with the bar, the tamales, and post-screening filmmaker talks.
_facts: Opened 1925 by M.A. Lightman Sr. as Hillsboro Theatre; oldest operating cinema in Tennessee.
The Grand Ole Opry broadcast from here 1934-1936, developing the two-performance-per-night format.
First film shown was D.W. Griffith's 'America'.
Purchased by a nonprofit coalition in 2007; renovated and reopened 2016.

s: An outdoor museum of Tennessee history carved into 19 acres of downtown — and it's free
w: The 19-acre linear park opened in 1996 to mark 200 years of Tennessee statehood and functions as an outdoor museum — 95 granite Tennessee county medallions, a 200-foot granite map of the state, a World War II memorial, a carillon, and a river of stars representing Tennessee's 3.5 million residents. French Lick Creek, which once ran through this site, now flows through a buried brick sewer beneath your feet. Most visited state park in Tennessee.
_facts: Opened June 1, 1996, Tennessee's bicentennial; 19 acres north of the State Capitol.
Contains 95 county medallions, a 200-foot granite state map, a WWII memorial, and a carillon.
Most visited of Tennessee's 65 state parks, receiving 2.5 million visitors annually.
French Lick Creek, historically important to Native Americans and early settlers, runs through a buried tunnel beneath the site.

s: The spot where Music Row was born — the Quonset Hut studio once stood here
w: Owen Bradley built a recording studio in a converted Quonset hut at 804 16th Avenue South in 1954 — the first music-industry business on what became Music Row. He produced Patsy Cline, Loretta Lynn, Buddy Holly, and Brenda Lee in that low corrugated shed. Columbia Records bought it, demolished it, and built the A-frame that replaced it. Today a bronze likeness of Bradley at his piano marks the site of where it all started.
_facts: Owen Bradley established Bradley Studios (the Quonset Hut) at 804 16th Avenue South in 1954 — first Music Row studio.
Bradley produced Patsy Cline, Loretta Lynn, and dozens of others here; the studio helped define the Nashville Sound.
He sold the studio to Columbia Records in 1962.
A bronze statue of Bradley at a piano now marks the site in the small park.

s: Barbara Mandrell's 27,000 sq ft log mega-mansion — one of the world's largest log homes
w: Barbara Mandrell built this three-storey, 20-room log mansion on 186 acres north of Nashville in 1988 and named it Fontanel — the soft spot on a baby's skull — wanting it to be a tender gathering place for her family. Indoor pool, shooting range, five fireplaces, two kitchens. The Mandrells moved out in 2002; the property reopened as a music-venue-and-resort complex in 2010. Tours still run through the house. The log walls are packed with Alabama band memorabilia.
_facts: Built 1988 for Barbara Mandrell; 27,000 sq ft on 186 acres in Whites Creek.
One of the largest log homes in the world; three stories, 20+ rooms, 13 baths.
Name 'Fontanel' refers to the soft spot on a baby's head.
Sold in 2002 and converted to a tourist and entertainment destination, opening in June 2010.

s: Andrew Jackson's plantation home — the man and the enslaved workers who built his legend
w: Jackson bought this 1,000-acre property in 1804 with nine enslaved people; by his death in 1845 the number had grown to 110, principally working the cotton fields. The Greek Revival mansion was rebuilt after a chimney fire in 1834; Jackson died here and is buried with his wife Rachel in the garden. The site is now a National Historic Landmark museum that, since the 2010s, has prominently told the stories of the enslaved community as well as the seventh president.
_facts: Andrew Jackson owned this 1,000-acre estate from 1804 until his death in 1845.
Enslaved population grew from 9 at purchase to 110 at Jackson's death; principal crop was cotton.
Current Greek Revival mansion rebuilt 1834-1837 after a chimney fire.
National Historic Landmark; Jackson and wife Rachel are buried in the garden.

s: Nashville craft distillery that made buckwheat whiskey before craft was cool
w: Nashville natives Derek Bell and Andrew Webber founded Corsair in Bowling Green in 2008, then brought it home to a WeHo industrial space. They make buckwheat whiskey, quinoa whiskey, smoked malts, barrel-aged gin, and absinthe — spirits so experimental they were winning awards before the American craft-distilling boom fully registered. Tasting room open to the public in the same building where everything is made.
_facts: Corsair Artisan Distillery founded 2008 in Bowling Green, Kentucky, by Nashville natives Derek Bell and Andrew Webber.
Known for innovative small-batch spirits including buckwheat whiskey, quinoa whiskey, and barrel-aged gin.
Nashville location at 601 Merritt Avenue in Wedgewood-Houston.

s: The Grand Ole Opry lived here for four years — the link in the chain between Belcourt and Ryman
w: Built in 1925 as Tennessee's World War I memorial, the 2,000-seat Beaux Arts hall became an Opry stage from 1939 to 1943, the penultimate stop before the Ryman. Received an architectural award at construction and was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2017. The adjacent War Memorial Plaza, flanked by the State Capitol and TPAC, is still Nashville's official ceremonial civic space.
_facts: Built 1925 as Tennessee's WWI memorial; 2,000-seat Beaux Arts auditorium.
Home of the Grand Ole Opry from 1939 to 1943, between its Belcourt Theatre and Ryman Auditorium eras.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2017.
Located adjacent to the Tennessee State Capitol.

s: 1930s Art Deco post office turned art museum — the postal counter in the basement still works
w: Marr and Holman designed this white marble Stripped Classicism post office in 1933-34 for $1.5 million, sited near Union Station so mail could move straight onto trains. The postal service outgrew downtown in 1986 and mostly vacated. The Frist Foundation spent $40 million converting it to an art centre, which opened in 2001. A working post office branch still occupies the basement level, accessible by its own entrance. No permanent collection; the exhibitions never stop.
_facts: Built 1933-34 as Nashville's main post office; Art Deco and Stripped Classicism style.
Located near Union Station for mail distribution via train.
Converted to the Frist Art Museum; opened 2001 after $40 million renovation funded by the Frist Foundation.
A functioning post office branch remains in the basement with its own entrance.

s: A railroad cattle lake inside Nashville city limits that became a wildlife sanctuary
w: The Louisville and Nashville Railroad dammed the hollow in 1914 to water steam locomotives and hold livestock shipped to market. L&N executives so loved the resulting lake that they declared it a wildlife sanctuary in 1923. When the railroad sold up in 1962, developers moved in; public pressure forced the state to buy it in 1973 as Tennessee's first State Natural Area. Otters, beavers, bald eagles, and bobcats — five miles from downtown.
_facts: Radnor Lake created 1914 by the Louisville and Nashville Railroad to water steam locomotives and livestock.
Declared a wildlife sanctuary in 1923 at the request of the Tennessee Ornithological Society.
State purchased the property in 1973, creating Tennessee's first State Natural Area.
1,368 acres with five miles of unpaved trails; home to river otters, beavers, and bald eagles.
